clinical_history: This ECG from an elderly woman shows which ONE of the following rhythms?
diagnosis: The rhythm is sinus tachycardia (P wave rate is about 100/min) with 2:1 (second degree) AV block.  In this case, the non-conducted (extra) sinus P waves on alternate beats simulate U waves located at the end of the T waves.The patient subsequently developed complete heart block and underwent permanent pacemaker implantation.The ECG also shows inferior and anterior Q waves suggesting prior myocardial infarctions. Of interest, multiple echocardiograms showed normal left ventricular (LV) function and no evidence of any wall motion abnormalities. Mild symmetric LV hypertrophy was present with a systolic LV outflow tract gradient raising the question of a hypertrophic cardiomyopathy variant which could account for Q waves in the absence of infarction.Note: Pure 2:1 AV block is considered a special category of second degree AV block and is not characterized as type 1 or type 2 since the block may be nodal or infra-nodal, even with a narrow (normal) QRS duration. In the latter instance, infra-nodal block, if present, is typically in the His area. The presence of typical AV Wenckebach in conjunction with episodes of  2:1 block argues for a nodal mechanism. Worsening block with increased sinus rate suggests an infranodal mechanism. Note that the mean QRS axis here is not sufficiently leftward to qualify for left anterior fascicular block (-45 degrees or more).
